original_abstract:
In this paper, we introduce **geo-indistinguishability**, a formal privacy framework for location-based systems (LBS) designed to protect a user’s precise coordinates while still facilitating the release of approximate location data required for service. This approach is a generalized adaptation of **differential privacy**, specifically tailored for the Euclidean metric. It ensures that the degree of privacy protection is proportional to a radius $r$, effectively making nearby locations indistinguishable to an untrusted server while allowing the server to discern broader geographic regions (e.g., distinguishing one city from another).

We propose a practical mechanism to achieve geo-indistinguishability by adding controlled noise drawn from a **planar Laplace distribution**. We address the critical implementation challenges of discretization and truncation, ensuring that the privacy guarantees remain robust when applied to digital grids and finite geographic areas. To demonstrate the real-world utility of our method, we show how it can be integrated into highly location-sensitive applications, such as point-of-interest retrieval, with negligible impact on bandwidth and service quality. Finally, we provide a comparative analysis against existing location-obfuscation techniques. Our results demonstrate that, among all mechanisms that operate independently of an adversary’s prior knowledge, our approach provides superior privacy guarantees for a given level of utility.
humanized_abstract:
In this paper, we introduce geo-indistinguishability as a formal privacy notion for location-based systems (LBS) ensuring the privacy of exact coordinates of a user while enabling the release of the imprecise locations needed to provide a service. The geo-indistinguishability notion is a generalization of differential privacy adapted to the Euclidean metric. Roughly, it offers a level of privacy proportional to a given radius r; that is, an unauthorized server will not be able to tell whether the user is at an exact location or at some other nearby location within the specified radius, while being able to tell the difference between larger locations such as cities.

Existing methods for achieving geo-indistinguishability apply noise sampled from the planar Laplace distribution. Discretization and truncation are addressed as a means of dealing with the digital grid and finite areas, and existing methods for location-obfuscation are compared to the proposed method. An application to a popular point-of-interest search service is presented with insights into possible implementations in terms of resource consumption and impact on communication throughput and service quality.

Existing methods for achieving geo-indistinguishability apply noise sampled from the planar Laplace distribution. An application to a popular point-of-interest search service is presented with insights into possible implementations in terms of resource consumption and impact on communication throughput and service quality.
document.input:
In this paper, we introduce **geo-indistinguishability**, a formal privacy framework for location-based systems (LBS) designed to protect a user’s precise coordinates while still facilitating the release of approximate location data required for service. This approach is a generalized adaptation of **differential privacy**, specifically tailored for the Euclidean metric. It ensures that the degree of privacy protection is proportional to a radius $r$, effectively making nearby locations indistinguishable to an untrusted server while allowing the server to discern broader geographic regions (e.g., distinguishing one city from another).

We propose a practical mechanism to achieve geo-indistinguishability by adding controlled noise drawn from a **planar Laplace distribution**. We address the critical implementation challenges of discretization and truncation, ensuring that the privacy guarantees remain robust when applied to digital grids and finite geographic areas. To demonstrate the real-world utility of our method, we show how it can be integrated into highly location-sensitive applications, such as point-of-interest retrieval, with negligible impact on bandwidth and service quality. Finally, we provide a comparative analysis against existing location-obfuscation techniques. Our results demonstrate that, among all mechanisms that operate independently of an adversary’s prior knowledge, our approach provides superior privacy guarantees for a given level of utility.
